revision.comment: [[上野王子]] を [[上野王子 (大阪市)]] へ移動: 同じく九十九王子社の一つで御坊市にある「上野王子 (御坊市)」との区別のため
revision.text: #REDIRECT [[上野王子 (大阪市)]]

revision.comment: Adding an intro to all of them. It has a magic word instead of a substitution because all of them are gonna be moved in the future to proper pagenames. See comment for more info.

''{{PAGENAME}}'' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. '''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] to [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[ja:上野王子]] revision.text:
'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| [[九十九王子]]一覧 |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:S]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| [[九十九王子]]一覧 |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:S]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:S]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:S]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] ==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]== {| class="navbox mw-collapsible" ! colspan="10" style="background:#33ff99" |{{Tnavbar-collapsible| |上野王子 (御坊市)}} |- | style="background:#99ffff" |（{{ill|Jōnan-gū|en}}{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|lt=Nachi Taisha}}） | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | | style="background:#FFFFFF" | |} [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]]]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | Image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | Location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:sqq]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Ueno Ōji (Gobo)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}][[Category:qq]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]]

revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. * Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). *Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]] Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:deprecated qq open]]

The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]]
