revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 1]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[category:qq close]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} - '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]]

It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]]

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (印南町)#津井王子}} [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]][[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5.== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (印南町)#津井王子}} '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (御坊市)#塩屋王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (印南町)#津井王子}} '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} : {{ill|Shioya Ōji (Gobo)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (御坊市)#塩屋王子|12=draft}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|Tsu'i Ōji (Inami)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (印南町)#津井王子|12=draft}} {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|12=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|12=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|12=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|12=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|12=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|12=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

revision.text:
'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft|12=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft|12=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft|12=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft|12=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|12=draft|12=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|12=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子|12=draft}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|12=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

'''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|qq=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|qq=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|1=Guanyin|2=ja|3=観音菩薩|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|1=Guanyin|2=ja|3=観音菩薩|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|qq=draft|qq=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|qq=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|qq=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:-てんふれえと]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)}}

Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|1=Guanyin|2=ja|3=観音菩薩|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|qq=draft|qq=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. '''Ueno Ōji (Gobo)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name=Ueno Ōji | image=[[File:Ichinono Oji haiden 2009-05-03.jpg|250px]]
Main Hall and Shrine Grove | location={{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | coordinates={{Coord|33|53|N|135|9|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|1=Guanyin|2=ja|3=観音菩薩|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Historical Rank=One of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established=Unknown | Designation=Gobo City Designated Historic Site }} '''Ueno Ōji''' (上野王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Gobo|ja|御坊市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines. The site of the shrine is marked with a sign for Ueno Ōji at the outskirts of the Ueno settlement in Nata-chō, just before the bridge crossing the Uenogawa River along the old road near [[:en:Japan_National_Route_42|Japan National Route 42]]. It was designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on May 8, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/20.html | title=Site of Ueno Ōji | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == History == While the exact founding date of Ueno Ōji is unknown, it appears in historical records dating back to the early 12th century. In the {{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an entry dated October 20, 1109 (Tennin 2) mentions a purification ritual performed at Ueno Slope. The shrine is first explicitly mentioned as "Next, Ueno Ōji" in an entry from October 10, 1201 (Kennin 1) in the "Kumano Dō no Aimagoki" (included in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Another record of a pilgrimage visit appears in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} dated April 26, 1210 (Jōgen 4).Heibonsha [1997: 105] In early modern records, it is noted that a Buddhist well (仏井戸) located along the Oguri Kaidō within a field was considered the original site of the shrine (as mentioned in the {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Three Buddhist statues—{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩|1=Guanyin|2=ja|3=観音菩薩|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|qq=draft|qq=draft}}Nishi [1987: 129]—located at the bottom of the well are believed to be the honji (本地仏) of the shrine. Pilgrims frequently visited this site, as noted in the {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Buddhist well still exists along the national road, and it is traditionally cleaned twice a year. The Buddhist well was also designated as a Gobo City Historic Site on July 18, 1969.{{Cite web | publisher=Gobo City Board of Education | url=http://www.city.gobo.wakayama.jp/gobo/1120ka/bunkazai/21.html | title=Buddhist Well | accessdate=2009-09-27 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Gobo City)|ja|九十九王子 (御坊市)|en|qq=draft|lt=Shioya Ōji|error=九十九王子 (御坊市)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} * '''Ueno Ōji''' - {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|九十九王子 (印南町)|qq=draft|lt=Tsu'i Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|oji=王子|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == '' Nishi, Ritsu (1987). ''Kumano Kōdō Michishirube - Current Survey of the Kumano Kujūku Ōji Sites''. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). '' Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook''. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 978-4882696292. '' Heibonsha (Ed.) (1997). ''Encyclopedia of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines''. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5. {{Kujūku Ōji}} {{translated page|ja|上野王子|version=34598366|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Municipalities in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Gobo, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]][[sn:-てんふれえと]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] }} [[sn:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[sn:-てんふれえと]] [[en:Ueno Ōji (Gobo)]] [[ja:上野王子]] [[ja:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[zh:上野王子 (御坊市)]] [[sn:eno Ōji (Gobo)]]
